channel.title: Writings on James Akl
channel.description: Recent content in Writings on James Akl

item.title: Lists
item.description: This page is under construction. Details, links, and content will be updated. I update this page regularly with resources and references. These may be helpful to other practitioners, researchers, or students. Lists & Dictionaries List of Algorithms List of Data Structures Outline of Algebraic Structures Outline of Machines Outline of Robotics Outline of Automation Dictionary of Algorithms & Data Structures Glossary of Higher Mathematical Jargon News & Blogs The AI Index Report Robohub Robotics Business Review The Robot Report Robotics-Worldwide Mailinglist – I recommend the ‘digest’ format Robotics Tomorrow Robots for Roboticists Towards Data Science Unite.

item.title: Reading Spatial Transforms
item.description: This article covers different ways to interpret homogeneous spatial transforms and provides appropriate notational practices for each. Homogeneous Matrices In the context of robot kinematics, a common modeling choice for forward kinematics is the use of homogeneous matrices. Recall the familiar homogeneous transformation matrix $\mathrm{T}$ as used in forward kinematics, $$ \mathrm{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathrm{R} & \mathbf{d} \\ \mathbf{0}& 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{xx'} & r_{xy'} & r_{xz'} & d_x \\ r_{yx'} & r_{yy'} & r_{yz'} & d_y \\ r_{zx'} & r_{zy'} & r_{zz'} & d_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

item.title: A Tour of Robot Mechanics
item.description: This article provides a conceptual but rigorous understanding of common robot kinematics and dynamics, presented in a forward and inverse problems setting. After careful reading, the ‘whys’ should become apparent, allowing the ‘hows’ to be better appreciated. Motivation The basic functionality we’d like robots to have, is to move. However, describing a motion from point $\mathsf{A}$ to point $\mathsf{B}$ precisely is not straightforward. Consider these two points $\mathsf{A}$ and $\mathsf{B}$.

item.title: Defining a Robot
item.description: In this article we will attempt to define a robot by examining the underlying conditions that distinguish robotic systems. It is impossible to satisfy all opinions with one definition, but being objectively right is not the goal. Instead, the aim is to better understand the nuances surrounding robots. History The word robot has literary origins (specifically, Czech) meaning forced labor, vaguely implying that a robot is a machine-slave. However, a more suitable metaphor for our modern times is that of a “helper”; specifically, that a robot is a machine-helper.
